description: The study of properties preserved under continuous deformations. Encompasses point-set topology (open sets, convergence, compactness) and algebraic topology (fundamental group, homology, homotopy).

concepts.name: Topological Space
concepts.description: A set X with a collection τ of subsets (open sets) satisfying: ∅,X∈τ; arbitrary unions of open sets are open; finite intersections of open sets are open. Generalizes the notion of 'neighborhood' beyond metric spaces.
references: Munkres, Topology, Ch.2; Armstrong, Basic Topology, Ch.1

concepts.name: Open and Closed Sets (Topology)
concepts.description: Open sets: elements of τ. Closed sets: complements of open sets. Interior int(A): largest open set in A. Closure cl(A): smallest closed set containing A. Boundary ∂A = cl(A)∩cl(Aᶜ).

concepts.name: Interior, Closure, and Boundary
concepts.description: int(A) = largest open subset of A. cl(A) = smallest closed set containing A. ∂A = cl(A)\int(A). Dense: cl(A)=X. Nowhere dense: int(cl(A))=∅. These operations characterize the topology.

concepts.name: Basis for a Topology
concepts.description: A collection ℬ of open sets such that every open set is a union of elements of ℬ. Example: open balls in a metric space form a basis. The order topology on ℝ uses open intervals as a basis. Second-countable: has a countable basis.

concepts.name: Continuity (Topology)
concepts.description: f:X→Y is continuous iff f⁻¹(V) is open in X for every open V in Y. Equivalent (for metric spaces) to the ε-δ definition. Composition of continuous maps is continuous. Continuity is a topological property.

concepts.name: Homeomorphism
concepts.description: A continuous bijection f:X→Y with continuous inverse. Homeomorphic spaces are topologically identical. Topological properties (compactness, connectedness, genus) are homeomorphism invariants. Classic examples: torus ≇ sphere; coffee cup ≅ donut.
references: Munkres, Topology, §2.4; Armstrong, Basic Topology, Ch.3

concepts.name: Compactness (Topology)
concepts.description: A space X is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Key properties: closed subsets of compact spaces are compact; continuous image of compact space is compact; compact Hausdorff spaces are normal. Extends Heine-Borel to abstract settings.

concepts.name: Tychonoff's Theorem
concepts.description: An arbitrary product of compact spaces is compact (in the product topology). Equivalent to the Axiom of Choice. Used in functional analysis (Banach-Alaoglu theorem). Most powerful compactness result.

concepts.name: Connectedness (Topology)
concepts.description: X is connected iff it cannot be written as a union of two disjoint non-empty open sets. Path-connected: any two points connected by a path. Path-connected ⟹ connected; converse fails. Components: maximal connected subsets.

concepts.name: Separation Axioms
concepts.description: Hierarchy: T₀ (topologically distinguishable), T₁ (points closed), T₂=Hausdorff (distinct points have disjoint neighborhoods), T₃=regular, T₄=normal (disjoint closed sets have disjoint open neighborhoods). Urysohn's lemma holds in normal spaces.

concepts.name: Quotient Space
concepts.description: Given f:X→Y, the quotient topology on Y is the finest topology making f continuous. Examples: circle S¹ = [0,1] with endpoints identified; torus = square with edges identified. Constructs new spaces from old by gluing.

concepts.name: Product Topology
concepts.description: Product topology on X×Y: subbasis of sets π₁⁻¹(U) and π₂⁻¹(V). Basis: U×V for open U⊆X, V⊆Y. Projections are continuous. Universal property: f:(Z,τ)→X×Y continuous iff each component is continuous.

concepts.name: Homotopy
concepts.description: Two continuous maps f,g:X→Y are homotopic if there exists a continuous H:X×[0,1]→Y with H(x,0)=f(x) and H(x,1)=g(x). Homotopy is an equivalence relation. Fundamental in algebraic topology and classifying spaces.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §1.0; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Homotopy Equivalence
concepts.description: X and Y are homotopy equivalent if ∃f:X→Y, g:Y→X with g∘f≃id_X and f∘g≃id_Y. Weaker than homeomorphism. S¹ and ℝ²\{0} are homotopy equivalent. Homotopy equivalences induce isomorphisms on homotopy and homology groups.

concepts.name: Fundamental Group
concepts.description: π₁(X,x₀) = homotopy classes of loops based at x₀, with concatenation. π₁(S¹)=ℤ; π₁(Sⁿ)=0 for n≥2; π₁(torus)=ℤ². The fundamental group classifies the 'holes' in 1D that loops can detect. Simply connected: π₁={e}.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.1; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Seifert-van Kampen Theorem
concepts.description: If X=U∪V with U,V open and U∩V path-connected, then π₁(X) is the amalgamated free product π₁(U)*_{π₁(U∩V)}π₁(V). Allows computing π₁ of a space from the π₁ of pieces. Computes π₁ of surfaces and CW complexes.

concepts.name: Covering Space
concepts.description: p:Ẽ→X is a covering map if every x∈X has an open neighborhood U such that p⁻¹(U) is a disjoint union of homeomorphic copies. Universal cover: simply connected covering space. Galois correspondence: subgroups of π₁(X) ↔ covering spaces.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §1.3; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Simplicial Complex
concepts.description: A simplicial complex: a collection of simplices (points, edges, triangles, tetrahedra, …) closed under faces. Provides a combinatorial way to study topological spaces. Every compact manifold admits a triangulation.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §2.1; Munkres, Elements of Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Homology
concepts.description: Assigns abelian groups Hₙ(X) (n-th homology groups) to topological spaces. H₀: connected components; H₁: loops not bounding discs; H₂: 2D holes. Functorial and homotopy invariant. Computable via simplicial/CW chain complexes.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.2; Munkres, Elements of Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Euler Characteristic
concepts.description: χ(X) = ∑(-1)ⁿ rank(Hₙ(X)) = V−E+F for polyhedra. Topological invariant: homeomorphic spaces have same χ. Sphere: χ=2; torus: χ=0; genus-g surface: χ=2−2g. Euler-Poincaré formula generalizes V−E+F=2.

concepts.name: Cohomology
concepts.description: Dual to homology: H^n(X) = Hom(Hₙ(X),ℤ) (universal coefficients). Has cup product structure making H*(X) a ring. De Rham cohomology: closed/exact differential forms. De Rham's theorem: de Rham ≅ singular (with ℝ coefficients).
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.3; Bott & Tu, Differential Forms in Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Topological Manifold
concepts.description: A Hausdorff, second-countable topological space where every point has a neighborhood homeomorphic to ℝⁿ. Examples: curves (1-manifolds), surfaces (2-manifolds), ℝⁿ, spheres Sⁿ. Classification of compact surfaces: connected sum of tori or projective planes.
references: Munkres, Topology, Ch.9; Lee, Introduction to Topological Manifolds

concepts.name: Smooth Manifold
concepts.description: A manifold with a compatible atlas of C∞ coordinate charts. Admits smooth functions, vector fields, differential forms, and tensors. Sard's theorem: set of critical values has measure zero. Foundation of differential topology and differential geometry.
references: Lee, Introduction to Smooth Manifolds; Guillemin & Pollack, Differential Topology
